segments.text: Hi everyone, welcome to the Sonar
Summit. My name is Kunal Kushwaha. I'm a
senior developer advocate at CAST AI and
I'm so excited to be here. Look at this
title by the way: Humans plus AI, not
humans versus AI. Because you know, right
now there's a lot of conversation around
AI replacing developers and what that
means for engineering teams and for
individuals as well. Today we're going
to cut through that hype. We're going to
look at some of the practical realities
of how engineering teams are actually
collaborating today. And I'm going to
show you what the data really says and
where the friction is hiding and also
how you can build a team that wins in
this era of AI. So no doom and no gloom.
Just some real talk. I'm very very
happy to be here. Let's get started. So
here's a road map for the next few
minutes. I have broken this down into
five key areas. The first one being the
reality gap. We're going to look at the
massive chasm between the AI hype and
what actually engineering data is
telling us. Spoiler alert, it's not
what the marketing brochures are saying.
So we're going to figure that out.
Next, we have to talk about human
responsibilities because what is the
unwavering core? The stuff
that no LLM, no matter how many
parameters it has, billions and
trillions of parameters, can actually
replace, there's always going to be that
human touch. Third is the friction
points. This is my favorite part because
we're going to talk about the 10-hour
paradox and the

junior developers scenario for them
like what they are facing and if you
felt like this whole AI thing
is sometimes making your job a little
bit harder this is for you.
Fourth we're going to learn how to adopt
strategies. So I'm going to give you
some actual frameworks, the dos and the
don'ts. So things to keep in mind, green
flags, red flags, and please for the
love of God, "don't" flags of AI
governance as well. And finally, the
hybrid intelligence. So what does the
dream team of the future look like? And
there's a lot to pack in. So let's
dive straight into the data. Let's start
with that first. So let's get real for a
second. You know, we have all seen
LinkedIn posts. I love LinkedIn,
it says that AI was supposed to turn
us all into like 10x engineers
overnight, but the narrative promised
this massive instant productivity
explosion, basically magic
transformation. But look at what is
actually happening in the trenches.
Organizations, as you can see on my
slides, using AI coding tools are seeing
roughly around 15% productivity
improvement. Don't get me wrong, 15% is
meaningful. I'm not saying that it's
not, but it's definitely not
revolutionary. Let me know what you
think in the live chat. Developers
themselves are rating the actual speed
boost at around 2.23
out of five and the overall cycle
times, how fast we actually
ship are staying pretty much flat.
So what is the vital takeaway from this?
We didn't get 10x engineers. We unlocked
like a 1.15x
engineering organization.
A little bit of progress. But that
distinction changes everything about how
we approach AI because if you try to
manage a team expecting 10x output,
you're going to burn them out and that's
not cool. You shouldn't do that.
But if you focus on how that 1.15x
gain scales across an entire
organization, that is where you actually
start to win. Now, I know I just burst
the bubble of 10x engineers. You
know, a 10x engineer isn't the one who
does 10x amount of work. It's the
engineer who makes other 10 people
better. Like that's the quote by Kelsey
Hightower. He said it better than me.
So, you can find it on one of his talks.
But don't go delete
your copilot subscriptions. I'm not
trying to demotivate you. So don't
do that yet. While this total
transformation hasn't happened, the
specific wins are actually kind of
mind-blowing when you look at the data
from places like Microsoft and
Azure. Check these numbers out. As you
can see, we are seeing like a 26%
increase in completed tasks. So that is
basically like So what is 26%? It's like
you get your Friday back, every single
week. And look at that 6x jump in
test generation. Nobody wakes up hyped up
to write boilerplate unit tests. If that's
you, then please reach out to me. I might
have some roles for you. AI
taking that off your plate is a massive
win for your own sanity because the
whole point is about giving developers
the time back to solve more problems.
There's so many problems in this world and
if AI can do these mundane tasks
for me then why not? But the real kicker
is that look at the far right: three
times more likely to generate top-tier
ideas.
There was a P&G study that found an
individual with AI could match the
creative output of a two-person human
team during ideation.
So this basically tells us something
significant: that AI is not just like
a typewriter. It's a brainstorming partner.
I am very with this statement because I use
AI for brainstorming quite a lot. So it's
not like replacing the engineer. It's
replacing drudgery.
So it's clearly cleaning up the busy work
out of the way so you can actually focus on
high-level design, creativity, and
problem solving that human beings
are pretty good at. So if you just
take away one thing from this talk
today, let it be this: the 10-hour
paradox. On the left side of the graph,
you can see that AI is doing exactly
what it promised. It's writing your
boilerplate. It's generating those unit
tests and it's basically handling and
giving you 10 hours of your life
back every single week. So you are coding
faster than ever. You are a rock star.
Life is good and everything is perfect.
But then the right side happens. You save
10 hours in the IDE. Very cool stuff.
But you are losing them immediately to
organizational friction. So you lose
them to Slack notifications and that
whole death loop. You lose them
because the requirements for a ticket
were so vague that you spent 2 hours
prompting an AI for something that
the business didn't even want. I have
done that. You lose them to tool
switching and broken CI/CD pipelines.
I've done that too. What is the hard
truth here? AI is just a faster way to
reach a bottleneck. So if your
process is broken, AI is not going to
fix it. It's just going to make the
crack in the foundation more obvious. Improving
the developer experience isn't just about
giving everyone a better LLM.
It's about cleaning up that friction so
we actually get to keep the time that AI
is saving us. Because you save 10 hours,
you spend 10 hours doing stuff that
shouldn't be requiring 10 hours.
Net gain is zero, sometimes negative. So
that is the 10-hour paradox which is
something I want you to think about
deeply. So AI is writing the code and
generating test cases. What do I do now?
Do I retire? I can't retire. I'm 27
years old. Am I just a prompt
engineer now? No. There is a set of core
responsibilities that AI simply cannot
touch because these responsibilities
require a soul and they are not just an
algorithm. So for example, intent and
context. AI can tell you how to build a
bridge but it has no idea why you are
building it. There are some trade-offs
as well. Every engineering decision is a
balancing act. Performance versus
readability, speed versus security.
Those aren't math problems; they are
value judgments. Also accountability:
when the site is going down at 3:00 a.m.
ChatGPT is not the one getting paged.
You are. So you own the legal, ethical,
professional consequences of that code.
Also, around 27 to 33% of AI generated
code has security vulnerabilities. That
is almost like one line in every three
lines of code. But if you're not being
replaced, then how is our day-to-day
actually changing? We are seeing three
major new roles emerge that I like to
call the human AI bridge. The first one:
we have the AI trainers. Think of these
folks like the mentors. They're not just
writing code. They are basically
curating the data and fine-tuning models
so that the AI actually understands your
specific domain and not just generic
patterns. Next, the AI explainers. This is
basically like a universal translator
role. You are bridging the gap between
a black box model and a stakeholder who
needs to know why they should trust a
specific output. It's about building
confidence through that context. And
finally, the AI sustainers. These are the
guardians. They are monitoring for drift,
bias, and making sure that the AI doesn't
go rogue on the organization's values.
Keeping things in check. The interesting
part is that these are not future sci-fi
roles. These are extensions of engineering
work that you are doing right now. It's
part of human-centered AI design, keeping
the person in the driver's seat while the
machines handle the heavy lifting. Let's
get into some stuff that people don't
usually mention in sales pitches. We
have four major hurdles here. The first
one is the trust gap. 60% of us don't
trust AI generated tests enough to ship
them and we end up double-checking every
line. So you're actually not saving any
time; you're just changing your bio to
"code auditor." There's the learning curve
tax. Management loves to skip that part.
But experienced developers slow down
by nearly 20% when they first start
using these tools. It's a short-term hit
that leadership must account for or they
just think the team is getting lazier,
which is not true. And let's be real, AI
was trained on beautiful modern GitHub
repositories. It absolutely freaks out
when it sees your 12-year-old legacy
monolith. That legacy mismatch is
actually quite real. But the biggest one
is the empathy gap. 63% of developers
feel like their leaders are just throwing
tools without understanding the actual
day-to-day grind. You can't just install
productivity from the top down. You have
to listen to people who are actually
writing the code. We need to talk about
the elephant in the room: I call it the
senior developer factory problem. Every
hiring manager wants a senior developer
with 10 years of experience. But here's
the paradox: seniors don't just spawn
into existence at level 50. They come
from junior developers, who come from
interns, who come from students. But
if you let AI do all the junior work, we
are cutting off that ladder. Look at the
choice a new developer faces today. On
one hand, they can lean on AI. They ship
fast. They hit their targets. But they
develop a shallow understanding; they
are shipping code they can't actually
explain or debug when things go sideways.
On the other hand, if they write manually
to actually learn the foundations, they
look slow compared to the AI augmented
developer. So the pressure to just use
the tool is massive. This is the most
underappreciated skill in AI adoption: if
we only optimize for today's productivity,
we are quietly dismantling tomorrow's
talent pipeline. We have to be intentional;
we need to create protected spaces
where juniors are allowed to be slow,
allowed to fail, and learn the "why"
and not just the "how." Allow me to
introduce you to GIST: GenAI Induced
Self-Admitted Technical Debt. Look at
this chart: we are seeing a massive spike
in debt where the developer knows that
the code is shaky but they ship it anyway.
This is not because developers have suddenly
become lazy. It is a signal that our
AI adoption is outpacing our comprehension
culture. We are so focused on getting
green check marks in the CI/CD pipeline
that we are forgetting a fundamental rule
of engineering: if you can't explain it,
you don't own it. We are essentially
trading long-term system health for a
5-minute quick win. If we want to win
with AI, we have to make sure that our
ability to understand code stays
faster than our ability to generate it.
So how do we actually do this without it
becoming a total mess? You can't just
flip a switch. You need a road map. I
have broken this down into three phases
that actually work in the real world.
Phase one is about the vibe check.
Don't force it on everyone at once. Find
those AI champions in your squad and
let them lead. Get your managers on
board so they are not surprised by the
20% learning tax and celebrate the small
wins early. Phase number two is the
cleanup. Get serious about quality. Use
custom lint rules to catch AI
hallucinations before they reach a pull
request. Standardize your context with
things like Cursor rules so the AI
actually knows what it's looking at. More
importantly, run surveys to see if
developers are actually trusting the tool
or just fighting with it. Phase three is
scaling. Stop doing guesswork; look at
the data, usage, activity, and sentiment.
Build context infrastructure so that
the AI is not just a generic chatbot,
but a partner that understands your
specific architecture. Remember that AI
adoption is not a sprint; it's a marathon.
If you're looking for a TL;DR for Monday,
this is it. On the "Do" slide: think of
AI as a very fast, very confident intern.
You must understand before you ship.
Scrutinize AI code more, not less. AI is
great at being mostly right, which is the
most dangerous kind of wrong. Keep your
manual coding skills sharp so your muscles
don't get rusty. For the "Avoid" list:
do not treat AI as a silver bullet. If
your processes were a mess before, AI
just helps you make a mess at 10 times
the speed. 88% of teams are using AI,
but only around 30% have a policy. We
have to move past vibe-based adoption.
Talk to your developers, listen to their
friction, and build a culture of
comprehension, not consumption.
Hybrid intelligence is about a division
of labor that makes sense. Let AI handle
scale, volume, repetition, and soul-crushing
boilerplate. Reclaim that space for
ambiguity, ethics, and architectural
vision. The winners are not the ones
who fire developers to save money, but
the ones who figure out how humans and
AI work together. When you get this
right, you don't just work faster; you
work on things that were literally
impossible 2 years ago. The code might
be generated, but the intent is yours.
Thank you for your time. Let's build
something great together. See you.
